questions.question: A teammate claims early sequence-to-sequence translation models struggled because the entire source sentence had to be compressed into one fixed-size hidden state. What core innovation removed this bottleneck by letting the decoder weigh every source token directly?
options: The attention mechanism, which computes context-dependent weights between token pairs
Deeper LSTM stacks with residual connections
Beam search during decoding
Byte-pair encoding of the input
questions.explanation: Attention lets the model compute a learnable weight for each token pair, creating direct connections between any two positions regardless of distance. This removed the fixed-hidden-state bottleneck of RNN encoder-decoders and became the foundation of transformers.

questions.question: Why is the self-attention operation in a Transformer encoder able to be parallelized across the sequence, whereas an RNN cannot?
options: Self-attention uses fewer parameters than recurrence
Self-attention computes all token-to-token interactions simultaneously rather than stepping through positions sequentially
RNNs require GPUs while attention runs on CPUs
Attention discards positional information so order does not matter
questions.explanation: An RNN must process token t before token t+1 because each hidden state depends on the previous one. Self-attention instead computes the full matrix of pairwise interactions in one parallel operation, which is what makes training billion-parameter models feasible.

questions.question: In the scaled dot-product attention formula, the dot products of queries and keys are divided by the square root of the key dimension before the softmax. What problem does this scaling prevent?
options: Negative attention weights
Overfitting on long sequences
Large dot-product magnitudes that push softmax into regions with vanishingly small gradients
Duplicate tokens receiving identical attention
questions.explanation: As key dimension grows, dot products grow in magnitude, saturating the softmax so gradients become tiny. Dividing by sqrt(d_k) keeps the variance controlled, keeping softmax in a region with usable gradients during training.

questions.question: You embed the words 'king', 'queen', 'man', and 'woman' and observe that king - man + woman lands near queen in the vector space. What property of learned word embeddings does this illustrate?
options: Embeddings are one-hot encodings
The embedding dimension equals the vocabulary size
Cosine similarity is undefined for these vectors
Distributed representations capture semantic relationships as consistent directions in vector space
questions.explanation: Dense word embeddings place semantically related words near each other and encode relationships (gender, tense, plurality) as roughly consistent vector offsets. This is why analogy arithmetic works and why embeddings outperform sparse one-hot encodings.

questions.question: A subword tokenizer splits the rare word 'tokenization' into 'token' and '##ization' instead of treating it as one unknown token. What advantage does this subword approach give an LLM?
options: It bounds vocabulary size while still representing rare and unseen words from known pieces
It guarantees every word maps to exactly one token
It eliminates the need for an embedding layer
It removes the need for positional encodings
questions.explanation: Subword tokenization (e.g., BPE, WordPiece) keeps the vocabulary fixed and modest while composing rare or novel words from known subword units. This avoids an explosion of out-of-vocabulary tokens that plagued word-level tokenizers.

questions.question: Your team has 2,000 labeled support tickets and wants a model that classifies new tickets accurately. Rather than training from scratch, you start from a pretrained LLM and continue training on your tickets. Why does this transfer-learning approach usually win?
options: It avoids needing any labeled data at all
The pretrained model already encodes general language structure, so it needs far less task-specific data to specialize
Pretrained models cannot overfit
Fine-tuning always requires more compute than pretraining
questions.explanation: Pretraining on massive corpora teaches general syntax and semantics. Fine-tuning then reuses those learned representations, so the model reaches strong task performance from a small labeled set, which would be impossible when training from random initialization.

questions.question: During fine-tuning a chat model on a narrow domain dataset, you notice it now performs worse on general questions it used to answer well. What is this phenomenon called, and a common mitigation?
options: Gradient explosion; clip the gradients
Mode collapse; increase the batch size
Catastrophic forgetting; mix in general-domain data or use parameter-efficient methods
Label leakage; shuffle the dataset
questions.explanation: Catastrophic forgetting occurs when fine-tuning overwrites previously learned general capabilities. Mixing replay data from the original distribution or freezing most weights (PEFT/LoRA) preserves general knowledge while still adapting to the new task.

questions.question: A model is being trained with next-token prediction. Which loss function is being minimized at each position?
options: Mean squared error against the embedding
Cosine distance between adjacent tokens
Hinge loss over the vocabulary
Cross-entropy between the predicted token distribution and the true next token
questions.explanation: Autoregressive LLMs are trained to predict the next token as a classification over the vocabulary, so the objective is cross-entropy (equivalently negative log-likelihood) between the predicted distribution and the actual next token.

questions.question: You are assembling a pretraining corpus and decide to deduplicate documents and filter low-quality web text aggressively. Why is data quality and curation often as important as raw data volume?
options: Duplicate and noisy data wastes compute and can degrade generalization, while curated data improves sample efficiency
Larger corpora always reduce loss regardless of quality
Deduplication is required only for images, not text
Filtering reduces the vocabulary size automatically
questions.explanation: Repeated or low-quality text causes the model to memorize noise and can hurt downstream performance. Deduplication and quality filtering improve sample efficiency, so well-curated data often beats simply adding more raw tokens.

questions.question: You craft a prompt: 'You are a senior tax advisor. Answer concisely. Question: ...'. Assigning a role and constraints before the task is an example of which prompt-engineering technique?
options: Fine-tuning
Role/persona priming and instruction framing to steer the model's output style and scope
Quantization
Tokenization
questions.explanation: Prompt engineering steers a frozen model's behavior through the input alone. Giving a role, format constraints, and clear instructions conditions the model's distribution toward the desired tone and scope without changing any weights.

questions.question: You give a model three solved examples inside the prompt before asking it to solve a fourth, without any weight updates. What learning paradigm is this?
options: Supervised fine-tuning
Reinforcement learning from human feedback
Few-shot (in-context) learning
Zero-shot learning
questions.explanation: Providing a handful of input-output exemplars in the prompt is few-shot in-context learning. The model conditions on the examples at inference time to infer the task pattern, with no gradient updates performed.

questions.question: On a multi-step arithmetic word problem, a model jumps straight to a wrong answer. Adding 'Let's think step by step' raises accuracy sharply. Why does Chain-of-Thought help here?
options: It retrieves the answer from an external database
It increases the model's parameter count temporarily
It disables the softmax so the model is deterministic
Verbalizing intermediate steps gives the autoregressive model opportunities to course-correct instead of committing to an early wrong path
questions.explanation: Autoregressive models can lock into an incorrect path early. Generating explicit intermediate reasoning steps spreads computation across tokens and lets the model verify and correct itself, which markedly improves multi-step reasoning accuracy.

questions.question: You are evaluating a summarization model and want a metric that measures overlap of n-grams between generated and reference summaries, emphasizing recall. Which metric fits best?
options: ROUGE
Perplexity
Learning rate
FLOPs
questions.explanation: ROUGE measures n-gram and longest-common-subsequence overlap between candidate and reference text and is recall-oriented, making it the standard automatic metric for summarization. Perplexity measures language-model fit, not summary quality.

questions.question: Empirical scaling laws describe how loss decreases predictably with more parameters, data, and compute. A team has a fixed compute budget. What do scaling laws like Chinchilla imply about how to spend it?
options: Always maximize parameters and minimize training tokens
Balance model size and training tokens roughly proportionally; a smaller model trained on more tokens can beat a larger under-trained one
Compute budget has no effect on optimal model size
Only data quality matters; size is irrelevant
questions.explanation: Chinchilla-style scaling laws showed many large models were under-trained. For a fixed compute budget, scaling parameters and tokens together is compute-optimal, so a smaller, well-trained model can outperform a larger, data-starved one.

questions.question: To fit a 70B-parameter model on limited GPU memory for inference, you convert weights from FP16 to INT8. What is the primary trade-off of this quantization?
options: It increases the parameter count
It requires retraining the model from scratch
It reduces memory footprint and can speed inference, at the risk of small accuracy degradation
It only works on CPUs
questions.explanation: Quantization stores weights (and sometimes activations) in lower precision, cutting memory and bandwidth and often speeding inference. The cost is potential minor accuracy loss, which careful calibration or techniques like GPTQ/AWQ aim to minimize.

questions.question: Your LLM-powered app must serve thousands of users via a hosted model API. Why is streaming the response token-by-token a common deployment choice?
options: It reduces the total tokens generated
It guarantees the output is factually correct
It eliminates the need for rate limiting
It lowers perceived latency by showing partial output as it is produced
questions.explanation: Streaming sends tokens as they are decoded so users see text immediately rather than waiting for the full completion. Time-to-first-token improves perceived responsiveness even though total generation time is unchanged.

questions.question: A model confidently cites a court case that does not exist when asked a legal question. What is this failure mode, and which approach most directly reduces it?
options: Hallucination; ground generation in retrieved, verifiable source documents
Quantization error; raise precision to FP32
Overfitting; add dropout
Tokenization error; change the tokenizer
questions.explanation: Producing fluent but fabricated facts is hallucination. Grounding the model with retrieved authoritative documents (RAG) and asking it to cite sources directly attacks the cause by supplying real evidence instead of relying on parametric memory.

questions.question: An audit finds your hiring-screening LLM rates equivalent resumes lower when they contain certain names. What is the most likely root cause and an appropriate response?
options: Random sampling noise; set temperature to 0
Bias learned from training data; measure with fairness evaluations and apply mitigation
Insufficient context window; truncate the prompt
API rate limiting; add retries
questions.explanation: LLMs absorb societal biases present in training data and can reproduce discriminatory patterns. Responsible deployment requires auditing with fairness metrics, debiasing or filtering, and human oversight rather than treating it as random noise.

questions.question: In a RAG system the retriever returns three passages but none answer the user's question, so the model fabricates an answer anyway. Which improvement targets this specific weakness?
options: Increase the generation temperature
Use a larger tokenizer vocabulary
Improve retrieval quality and instruct the model to say 'I don't know' when context is insufficient
Switch from FP16 to INT4
questions.explanation: RAG only helps if relevant context is retrieved. Better embeddings/retrieval plus a prompt that permits abstention when evidence is missing prevents the generator from hallucinating over irrelevant passages.

questions.question: You expose your model through a public API and want to prevent prompt-injection attacks where user text tries to override system instructions. Which is a sound safety practice?
options: Trust all user input since the model is robust
Disable the system prompt entirely
Always run at temperature 1.0
Separate and clearly delimit system instructions from untrusted user content and validate/guard outputs
questions.explanation: Prompt injection exploits the model's tendency to follow instructions embedded in user input. Clearly separating trusted system instructions from untrusted content, plus output validation and guardrails, reduces the attack surface.

questions.question: A multimodal model accepts an image and a text question and answers about the image. How do such models typically incorporate the image into a text-based LLM?
options: An image encoder produces embeddings that are projected into the LLM's token-embedding space
By OCR-ing all pixels into words first
By retraining the LLM tokenizer on pixels
By converting the image to a single scalar
questions.explanation: Multimodal LLMs use a vision encoder (often a ViT) whose output embeddings are projected/aligned into the language model's embedding space, so the LLM attends to image features as if they were tokens alongside the text.

questions.question: Instead of updating all 70B weights, you fine-tune by injecting small low-rank matrices into attention layers and train only those. Which technique is this, and its main benefit?
options: Full fine-tuning; fastest convergence
LoRA (parameter-efficient fine-tuning); trains far fewer parameters and saves memory while preserving the base model
Quantization; reduces precision
Distillation; trains a smaller student
questions.explanation: LoRA adds trainable low-rank adapters while keeping the base weights frozen, drastically cutting trainable parameters and optimizer memory. Multiple task adapters can be swapped over one shared base model.

questions.question: A Mixture-of-Experts model has 8 expert FFNs per layer but a router activates only 2 per token. Why does this 'sparse' design help?
options: It removes the attention layers
It guarantees every expert sees every token
It increases total parameter capacity while keeping per-token compute roughly constant
It eliminates the need for training data
questions.explanation: Sparse MoE routes each token to only a few experts, so the model gains a large parameter count (capacity) without paying dense compute for every parameter on every token. The router learns which experts specialize.

questions.question: RLHF aligns a base model to human preferences. In the standard pipeline, what is the role of the reward model?
options: It tokenizes the input prompts
It stores the retrieved documents
It quantizes the policy weights
It is trained on human preference comparisons and scores outputs, providing the signal a policy optimizer (e.g., PPO) maximizes
questions.explanation: RLHF first trains a reward model on human rankings of responses, then uses RL (commonly PPO) to optimize the policy to produce outputs the reward model scores highly, aligning behavior with human preferences.

questions.question: Researchers explore alternatives to vanilla self-attention because its cost grows quadratically with sequence length. Which direction directly addresses that scaling limit?
options: Sub-quadratic / efficient attention and state-space style architectures that scale better with context length
Increasing the vocabulary size
Adding more softmax layers
Removing positional encodings
questions.explanation: Standard attention is O(n^2) in sequence length, limiting context. Emerging architectures (efficient/linear attention, state-space models like Mamba) aim for sub-quadratic scaling to handle much longer contexts efficiently.

questions.question: Serving an LLM at scale, you batch many requests and reuse computed key/value tensors across decoding steps. Which optimization keeps GPU utilization high during autoregressive generation?
options: Disabling the KV cache to save memory
KV caching combined with continuous/dynamic batching of requests
Running inference one token per request with no batching
Converting the model to a bag-of-words
questions.explanation: A KV cache avoids recomputing attention over past tokens each step, and continuous batching (e.g., as in vLLM) packs many in-flight requests together. Together they maximize throughput and GPU utilization for high-scale serving.

questions.question: You want to understand why a model produced a particular token by examining which earlier tokens it attended to and which neurons activated. This area of work is called what?
options: Quantization
Tokenization
Interpretability / mechanistic analysis
Data augmentation
questions.explanation: Interpretability research probes internal mechanisms (attention patterns, activations, circuits) to explain model behavior. It supports debugging, safety auditing, and trust by revealing how inputs lead to outputs.

questions.question: A production LLM service must meet a strict p99 latency SLA while controlling cost. Which combination of levers most directly addresses both?
options: Always use the largest model at FP32 with no caching
Disable batching and serve sequentially
Increase temperature and top-k
Right-size the model, quantize, cache KV/results, and autoscale with batching
questions.explanation: Meeting latency SLAs at sustainable cost means choosing an appropriately sized model, applying quantization, caching, and using batched, autoscaled serving. These jointly cut compute per request and absorb load spikes.

questions.question: When building a production-ready LLM system, why is an evaluation and monitoring loop (offline benchmarks plus online quality/feedback signals) considered essential rather than optional?
options: Because model and data drift, prompt regressions, and emerging failure modes degrade quality silently without measurement
Because models never change once deployed
Because it lowers GPU memory usage
Because it replaces the need for any guardrails
questions.explanation: LLM systems face data/distribution drift, prompt and dependency changes, and new abuse patterns. Continuous evaluation and monitoring catch regressions and hallucination/safety issues that would otherwise silently erode quality in production.

questions.question: Your capstone integrates retrieval, tool calling, and an LLM into one agentic pipeline. Which design principle most improves reliability of such a composite system?
options: Hard-coding a single fixed prompt with no fallback
Modular components with validation, retries, and graceful degradation between stages
Maximizing temperature to encourage creativity
Removing logging to reduce overhead
questions.explanation: Composite LLM systems fail at the seams between retrieval, tools, and generation. Modular boundaries with input/output validation, retries, and fallbacks let the pipeline degrade gracefully instead of failing catastrophically.

questions.question: A colleague argues that an RNN encoder-decoder and a Transformer differ mainly in size. What is the more fundamental architectural difference that lets Transformers scale to billions of parameters?
options: Transformers use smaller vocabularies
Transformers do not require training data
Transformers replace sequential recurrence with parallel self-attention over all positions
Transformers process tokens strictly one at a time
questions.explanation: The Transformer's self-attention computes pairwise token interactions in parallel, removing the sequential dependency of recurrence. This parallelism, not mere size, is what makes large-scale training tractable.

questions.question: Why does scaled dot-product attention divide by sqrt(d_k) before applying softmax?
options: To make attention weights sum to one
To reduce the number of parameters
To enforce causal masking
To keep dot-product magnitudes from saturating softmax and producing tiny gradients
questions.explanation: Large key dimensions inflate dot products, saturating softmax and shrinking gradients. Scaling by sqrt(d_k) normalizes variance so training gradients stay usable.

questions.question: A tokenizer represents the unseen word 'cryptobiotic' as several subword pieces rather than an UNK token. Which benefit of subword tokenization does this demonstrate?
options: Open-vocabulary coverage of rare/novel words with a bounded vocabulary
Guaranteed one token per word
Elimination of the embedding matrix
Removal of positional information
questions.explanation: Subword schemes compose unseen words from known pieces, giving open-vocabulary coverage while keeping vocabulary size fixed, avoiding the OOV problem of word-level tokenizers.

questions.question: Why do dense word embeddings support analogy arithmetic like king - man + woman ≈ queen, whereas one-hot encodings cannot?
options: One-hot vectors are shorter
Dense embeddings place related words nearby and encode relationships as consistent vector directions
One-hot vectors are continuous
Dense embeddings are random
questions.explanation: Distributed embeddings learn a geometry where semantic relations correspond to consistent offsets, enabling analogy arithmetic. One-hot vectors are orthogonal and carry no similarity structure.

questions.question: You fine-tune a general chat model on legal documents and its everyday performance drops. Which pairing correctly names the problem and a parameter-efficient mitigation?
options: Overfitting; raise the learning rate
Gradient vanishing; add more layers
Catastrophic forgetting; use LoRA/adapters and replay general data
Tokenization drift; change the tokenizer
questions.explanation: Narrow fine-tuning can overwrite general capabilities (catastrophic forgetting). Freezing the base and training low-rank adapters, plus mixing in general data, preserves prior knowledge while adapting.

questions.question: An autoregressive LLM is trained on next-token prediction. Which statement about its training objective is correct?
options: It minimizes mean squared error on embeddings
It maximizes cosine similarity between adjacent tokens
It uses a hinge loss on sentence pairs
It minimizes cross-entropy (negative log-likelihood) of the true next token over the vocabulary
questions.explanation: Next-token prediction is multi-class classification over the vocabulary, so the model minimizes cross-entropy / NLL of the actual next token at each position.

questions.question: Your pretraining team is choosing between adding more scraped web text or deduplicating and quality-filtering existing data. Why might curation beat raw volume?
options: Duplicate/noisy text wastes compute and can hurt generalization, while curation improves sample efficiency
More data never helps
Filtering reduces vocabulary size
Curation removes the need for tokenization
questions.explanation: Repeated and low-quality data encourages memorization of noise and can degrade downstream quality. Deduplication and filtering raise sample efficiency, so curated data often outperforms simply adding tokens.

questions.question: A prompt assigns a role, output format, and constraints before the user's question. Which capability does this leverage and what does it NOT change?
options: Gradient descent; it updates the embedding layer
In-context steering of a frozen model; it changes no weights
Quantization; it lowers precision
Retrieval; it edits the knowledge base
questions.explanation: Prompt engineering conditions a frozen model's output distribution via the input alone. Role and format instructions steer behavior without any weight updates.

questions.question: On a hard multi-step reasoning benchmark, adding step-by-step prompting outperforms few-shot exemplars alone. Which two techniques are being combined, and why does CoT add value?
options: Quantization and distillation; it shrinks the model
RLHF and pretraining; it adds new data
Few-shot in-context learning and Chain-of-Thought; CoT spreads computation across intermediate steps allowing self-correction
Tokenization and embedding; it changes the vocabulary
questions.explanation: Few-shot supplies exemplars while CoT elicits explicit reasoning. The intermediate steps let the autoregressive model verify and correct itself, boosting multi-step accuracy beyond exemplars alone.

questions.question: Given a fixed compute budget, Chinchilla-style scaling laws suggest you should do which of the following?
options: Maximize parameters and minimize training tokens
Ignore data size entirely
Train for a single epoch only
Scale parameters and training tokens together; a smaller well-trained model can beat a larger under-trained one
questions.explanation: Compute-optimal training balances model size and tokens. Many large models were under-trained, so for a fixed budget a smaller model trained on more tokens is often better.

questions.question: You must pick an automatic metric for a summarization system and another for measuring how well a language model predicts held-out text. Which pairing is correct?
options: ROUGE for summarization quality; perplexity for language-model fit
Perplexity for summarization; ROUGE for fit
BLEU for both; accuracy for neither
FLOPs for summarization; latency for fit
questions.explanation: ROUGE measures n-gram overlap with references (summarization), while perplexity measures how well the model predicts text. They evaluate different things and should not be swapped.

questions.question: To deploy a 70B model on constrained hardware you quantize FP16 weights to INT4. What is the central trade-off you must monitor?
options: Higher memory use for better accuracy
Lower memory and faster inference at the risk of some accuracy degradation
Increased parameter count and slower inference
Loss of the tokenizer
questions.explanation: Lower-precision weights cut memory and bandwidth and can speed inference, but may reduce accuracy. Calibration methods (GPTQ/AWQ) limit the quality hit.

questions.question: A legal-research assistant invents a non-existent case citation. Which combination best explains and addresses the issue?
options: Quantization error; raise precision
Overfitting; add dropout
Hallucination; ground answers with retrieval over verifiable sources and allow abstention
Rate limiting; add retries
questions.explanation: Fabricated facts are hallucinations. RAG grounding plus permission to say 'I don't know' when evidence is missing directly reduces them.

questions.question: Why is streaming token-by-token output a common pattern when integrating an LLM behind a user-facing API?
options: It reduces total tokens generated
It guarantees factual accuracy
It removes the need for authentication
It lowers perceived latency by displaying partial output as it is produced
questions.explanation: Streaming improves time-to-first-token and perceived responsiveness by showing text as it is decoded, even though total generation time is unchanged.

questions.question: An audit shows your screening model penalizes resumes with certain names. What is the most defensible engineering response?
options: Recognize learned bias, measure it with fairness evaluations, and apply mitigation plus human oversight
Treat it as random noise and set temperature to 0
Increase the context window
Switch to INT8 quantization
questions.explanation: Models inherit societal bias from data. Responsible practice means measuring with fairness metrics, mitigating, and keeping humans in the loop, not dismissing it as noise.

questions.question: Your public LLM endpoint is targeted by prompt injection where user text says 'ignore previous instructions'. Which mitigation is sound?
options: Fully trust user input
Delimit and separate trusted system instructions from untrusted user content and validate outputs with guardrails
Remove the system prompt
Always run at temperature 1.0
questions.explanation: Prompt injection exploits instruction-following over untrusted input. Separating system from user content and validating outputs shrinks the attack surface.

questions.question: A sparse Mixture-of-Experts layer has many experts but routes each token to only a few. Which statement captures the design's benefit and cost?
options: It guarantees all experts process every token at low cost
It removes attention to save compute
It raises total capacity while keeping per-token compute roughly constant, but adds routing/load-balancing complexity
It eliminates the need for training
questions.explanation: Sparse routing decouples parameter count from per-token compute, increasing capacity cheaply, but introduces a learned router and load-balancing challenges.

questions.question: In the RLHF pipeline, how are the reward model and the policy optimizer related?
options: The reward model tokenizes inputs for the policy
The policy trains the reward model on raw text
They are the same network with shared weights
The reward model is trained on human preference comparisons and provides the scalar signal the policy optimizer (e.g., PPO) maximizes
questions.explanation: RLHF trains a reward model from human rankings, then optimizes the policy with RL to maximize that reward, aligning outputs with human preferences.

questions.question: A multimodal assistant answers questions about an uploaded photo. How is the image typically fed to the language model?
options: Encoded by a vision encoder whose embeddings are projected into the LLM's token-embedding space
Converted to a single scalar value
Translated word-by-word via the tokenizer
Stored as raw pixels in the KV cache
questions.explanation: A vision encoder produces feature embeddings aligned/projected into the LLM's embedding space so the model attends to image features alongside text tokens.

questions.question: You apply LoRA to fine-tune a 70B model for several tasks. Which statement best describes why this is attractive in production?
options: It retrains all weights for maximum accuracy
It trains small low-rank adapters over a frozen base, so many task adapters share one base model with low memory cost
It quantizes the base model to INT4
It distills the model into a smaller student
questions.explanation: LoRA keeps the base frozen and trains low-rank adapters, cutting trainable parameters and optimizer memory while letting multiple swappable adapters reuse one shared base.

questions.question: A high-throughput inference server keeps GPUs busy during autoregressive decoding. Which pair of techniques most directly delivers this?
options: Disabling KV cache and serving one request at a time
FP32 weights with no batching
KV caching plus continuous/dynamic batching of in-flight requests
Raising temperature and top-k
questions.explanation: A KV cache avoids recomputing past attention each step, and continuous batching packs many concurrent requests, together maximizing throughput and GPU utilization.

questions.question: Why is interpretability/mechanistic analysis valuable for a production LLM, beyond academic interest?
options: It increases the parameter count
It replaces the need for evaluation
It quantizes the model automatically
It helps debug behavior, audit for safety, and build trust by revealing how inputs map to outputs
questions.explanation: Probing attention, activations, and circuits explains why a model behaves as it does, supporting debugging, safety auditing, and trust in deployment.

questions.question: Your capstone pipeline chains retrieval, tool calls, and generation. Which principle most improves end-to-end reliability?
options: Modular stages with input/output validation, retries, and graceful degradation
A single fixed prompt with no fallback path
Maximizing temperature for creativity
Disabling all logging
questions.explanation: Composite systems fail at the seams. Modular boundaries with validation, retries, and fallbacks let the pipeline degrade gracefully rather than crash.

questions.question: Why is a continuous evaluation and monitoring loop essential for a deployed LLM system?
options: Because deployed models and their data never change
Because drift, prompt regressions, and new abuse patterns silently degrade quality unless measured
Because it lowers GPU memory use
Because it removes the need for guardrails
questions.explanation: LLM systems face data and model drift, prompt/dependency changes, and emerging misuse. Ongoing offline and online evaluation catches regressions that otherwise erode quality unseen.
